title: Cost of Going Deep
concept: Deep learning can be powerful, but the tradeoff is cost: it usually needs more labeled data, more compute, longer training, more tuning, and can be harder to understand when something goes wrong.

exercises.prompt: Recap: When does deep learning often pull ahead?

options.text: When there is lots of raw, complex data like images, audio, or large text collections
When a problem has only two rows and one obvious column
When you want the quickest model with the least tuning every time

exercises.explain: Exactly. Deep learning can be powerful — but it is rarely the cheapest or simplest option.

cards.title: The model is still training...
cards.body: One of the first surprises in deep learning is how much waiting can be involved. A classical model might train in seconds or minutes. A deep model might need hours, days, or repeated experiments before you even know whether your setup is good.

cards.title: What is the cost of going deep?
cards.body: The cost is not just money. Deep learning often needs more labeled data, stronger hardware like GPUs, more training time, more settings to tune, and more patience when debugging. You may gain power, but you usually pay for it in complexity.

cards.title: A race car, not a bicycle
cards.body: A race car can go much faster than a bicycle — but it needs fuel, tuning, spare parts, and a skilled pit crew. Deep learning can reach higher performance on some tasks, but it usually asks for more support around it.

cards.title: Two projects, two timelines
cards.body: A student can train a small tabular model on a laptop during lunch. Training an image model may require batches of data, a GPU, many epochs, and careful monitoring overnight. If results are bad, the problem could be the data, the architecture, the learning rate, or something else entirely.

cards.title: Bigger is not automatically better
cards.body: It is easy to assume that a deeper model must be the smartest choice. Not true. If the dataset is small, the features are already clear, or the project needs fast iteration, a simpler classical model may be the better engineering decision.

cards.title: Classical speed vs. deep power
cards.body: One tradeoff shows up again and again:

left.text:
Less data hungry
Faster experiments
Easier to inspect

right.text:
More data and compute
Longer training loops
Harder debugging

exercises.prompt: Which tradeoff is most strongly associated with deep learning?

options.text: It often needs more data and more compute to train well
It guarantees instant training on any laptop
It makes debugging easier than a simple linear model

exercises.explain: Correct. Deep learning's power often comes bundled with heavier training demands.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a deep model performs poorly, it is usually easier to debug than a simple linear model.
exercises.explain: False. Deep models usually have more moving parts, which can make debugging slower and less obvious.

exercises.sentence: Training deep models often takes longer because there are more weights and more ___ to tune.
exercises.explain: Exactly. More knobs to adjust means more experiments to run.

exercises.prompt: Which situation best shows the cost of going deep?

options.text: A team waits overnight for GPU training, then still has to figure out whether the issue is the data, architecture, or tuning
A student reads a spreadsheet column name
A rule says a door opens if you push it

exercises.explain: Right. Longer experiments and harder debugging are classic deep learning tradeoffs.

exercises.prompt: A robotics club has 5,000 rows of clean sensor data, one laptop, and one weekend. Should they start with a deep network or a simpler classical model, and why?

options.text: A simpler classical model, because the deep learning cost is probably not worth it for a small clean table under tight compute and time limits
A deep network, because bigger models are always better
A deep network, because deep learning never needs tuning
Either one, because compute and training time do not matter

exercises.explain: Exactly. Deep learning is not the default answer to everything. And to understand what these powerful models are really built from, we are about to zoom all the way down to the simplest possible machine decisions.
